title: Iteration

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Iteration; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Iteration
sources.claim: A loop needs an initial state, a continuation condition or next element, a body, progress toward completion, and a clear state when repetition stops.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A loop needs an initial state, a continuation condition or next element, a body, progress toward completion, and a clear state when repetition stops.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A counted loop visits indexes zero through length minus one exactly once and never reads the nonexistent index equal to length. Your goal is to design and trace loops while identifying initialization, condition, update and termination. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Expressions and evaluation; Selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A loop needs an initial state, a continuation condition or next element, a body, progress toward completion, and a clear state when repetition stops. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Empty input, one iteration, off-by-one bounds, mutation during traversal, and conditions that never change are distinct cases that can break termination or coverage. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A loop terminates automatically once its body has run enough times, even if the condition never changes.

includes: Use counted and condition-controlled repetition with correct progress.
Design and trace loops while identifying initialization, condition, update and termination.
excludes: Empty input, one iteration, off-by-one bounds, mutation during traversal, and conditions that never change are distinct cases that can break termination or coverage.

outcomes: Design and trace loops while identifying initialization, condition, update and termination.

examples.title: Iteration: direct case
examples.situation: A counted loop visits indexes zero through length minus one exactly once and never reads the nonexistent index equal to length.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A counted loop visits indexes zero through length minus one exactly once and never reads the nonexistent index equal to length.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Identify and test the progress measure on every path, then prove that the continuation condition eventually becomes false or input is exhausted.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Identify and test the progress measure on every path, then prove that the continuation condition eventually becomes false or input is exhausted.

examples.title: Iteration: boundary case
examples.situation: A password retry loop stops on success or after three failed attempts, updating the attempt count on every failed path.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A password retry loop stops on success or after three failed attempts, updating the attempt count on every failed path.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Empty input, one iteration, off-by-one bounds, mutation during traversal, and conditions that never change are distinct cases that can break termination or coverage.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A loop terminates automatically once its body has run enough times, even if the condition never changes.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and trace loops while identifying initialization, condition, update and termination.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A loop terminates automatically once its body has run enough times, even if the condition never changes.
misconceptions.correction: Identify and test the progress measure on every path, then prove that the continuation condition eventually becomes false or input is exhausted.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A loop terminates automatically once its body has run enough times, even if the condition never changes.

analogy.example: A route with ordered checkpoints, forks, and repeated laps can model Iteration while each decision uses the current conditions.
usefulFor: Visualizing order, branching, repetition, progress, and nested routes
limitations: Real control flow can include calls, exceptions, concurrency, and evaluation rules absent from a physical route.
Empty input, one iteration, off-by-one bounds, mutation during traversal, and conditions that never change are distinct cases that can break termination or coverage.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

remediation.retryPrompt: Re-evaluate this case after applying the correction, then state the decisive evidence: A password retry loop stops on success or after three failed attempts, updating the attempt count on every failed path.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and trace loops while identifying initialization, condition, update and termination. Keep this boundary in view: Empty input, one iteration, off-by-one bounds, mutation during traversal, and conditions that never change are distinct cases that can break termination or coverage.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Iteration without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: A loop terminates automatically once its body has run enough times, even if the condition never changes.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A password retry loop stops on success or after three failed attempts, updating the attempt count on every failed path.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Nested control and tables”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
